name: Peter Steinberger
bio:
Peter Steinberger is an Austrian software engineer, entrepreneur, and the creator of OpenClaw, one of the fastest-growing open-source projects in GitHub history. Born in rural Upper Austria, he became obsessed with computers at age 14 and went on to study medical computer science at TU Wien, where he launched the university's first Mac and iOS developer course. In 2011, while waiting for a U.S. work visa, he co-founded PSPDFKit with Martin Schurrer, bootstrapping a PDF rendering SDK that grew to power document workflows at Dropbox, Lufthansa, IBM, and over one billion Apple devices. The company raised over EUR 100M from Insight Partners in October 2021 and later rebranded as Nutrient.

After exiting PSPDFKit, Steinberger spent three years away from coding, recovering from severe burnout he described as 'complete identity loss, not just fatigue.' In late 2024 he discovered AI-assisted development and called it his 'holy f--- mind-blowing moment.' In November 2025, he built a one-hour prototype connecting WhatsApp to Claude's API that became Clawdbot. The bot autonomously added voice message support by inspecting file headers and routing audio through ffmpeg and Whisper. After a polite trademark concern from Anthropic, the project was renamed MoltBot and then OpenClaw. Within three months it reached over 200,000 GitHub stars and two million visitors in a single week, making it one of the fastest growth curves in GitHub history.

On February 14, 2026, Steinberger announced he was joining OpenAI to 'build an agent that even my mum can use,' rejecting offers from Meta where Zuckerberg had reached out personally. OpenClaw transitioned to an independent open-source foundation sponsored by OpenAI. Today the project has over 356,000 GitHub stars and a thriving ecosystem including ClawhHub (a skill directory), Lobster (a workflow shell), and ACPX (an Agent Client Protocol CLI). Steinberger describes himself as the 'Clawdfather' and advocates for 'agentic engineering' over 'vibe coding,' emphasizing that AI replaces repetitive plumbing while developers become architects, editors, and prompt engineers.

executive_summary.one_liner: Creator of OpenClaw (356K+ GitHub stars), the open-source AI personal assistant that became one of the fastest-growing projects in GitHub history, now at OpenAI building the next generation of personal agents.
key_facts: Built OpenClaw from a one-hour WhatsApp-to-Claude prototype in Nov 2025 to 356K+ GitHub stars
Previously founded PSPDFKit, bootstrapped to 1B+ devices, exited for EUR 100M+ in 2021
Joined OpenAI in Feb 2026 to drive personal agent development; OpenClaw moved to independent foundation
Orchestrates 5-50 parallel AI agents daily, merging 600+ commits; calls PRs 'prompt requests'
OpenClaw ecosystem: main app (356K stars), ClawhHub skills directory (7.9K), Lobster workflow shell (1.1K), ACPX agent protocol CLI (2.1K)
47K+ GitHub followers, 385K X followers; based in Vienna & London
executive_summary.career_arc: From rural Austria to founding a bootstrapped PDF SDK used on a billion devices, through severe post-exit burnout, to an explosive comeback building the most-starred AI assistant on GitHub and joining OpenAI.
executive_summary.current_focus: Working at OpenAI on bringing personal agents to mainstream users, while OpenClaw continues under independent foundation governance with OpenAI sponsorship.
executive_summary.industry_significance: Steinberger demonstrated that a single developer using AI-assisted coding can build a project that rivals teams of hundreds. OpenClaw's growth validated the market for open-source, locally-run AI personal assistants that integrate with messaging platforms.

timeline.event: Co-founded PSPDFKit with Martin Schurrer, bootstrapping a PDF SDK for iOS
PSPDFKit raised EUR 100M+ from Insight Partners; Steinberger sold shares and stepped back
Returned to coding after three years of burnout; discovered AI-assisted development
Published Clawdbot (later OpenClaw) on GitHub as a WhatsApp-to-Claude AI assistant prototype
Created Peekaboo for macOS screenshot and GUI automation, and canvas for browser-based agent interaction
Launched summarize (5.5K+ stars): point at any URL/YouTube/podcast and get the gist
Founded the OpenClaw GitHub organization; launched openclaw.ai and ClawhHub skill directory
Launched ClawhHub skills archive and community skill ecosystem
Created Lobster, an OpenClaw-native workflow shell for composable pipelines and automations
Renamed Clawdbot to MoltBot following Anthropic trademark concern
Final rename to OpenClaw; project reached 100K+ GitHub stars in roughly three months
Announced joining OpenAI to drive personal agent development; OpenClaw transitions to independent foundation
Launched ACPX, a headless CLI client for stateful Agent Client Protocol sessions (2.1K+ stars)

key_contributions.title: OpenClaw
key_contributions.description: Open-source, MIT-licensed AI personal assistant running locally on any OS. Built in TypeScript and Swift, it operates through WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, and Signal, executing real tasks: email management, browser control, shell commands, workflow scheduling, and PR triage. 356K+ GitHub stars, 72K+ forks, 19K+ org followers. One of the fastest-growing open-source projects in GitHub history.

key_contributions.title: ClawhHub
key_contributions.description: Community-driven skill directory for OpenClaw, enabling users to discover, share, and install agent skills. The ecosystem has grown to thousands of community-contributed skills archived in the skills repository. 7.9K stars.

key_contributions.title: ACPX
key_contributions.description: Headless CLI client for stateful Agent Client Protocol (ACP) sessions, enabling programmatic interaction with AI agents from the terminal. 2.1K+ stars.

key_contributions.title: Lobster
key_contributions.description: An OpenClaw-native workflow shell: a typed, local-first macro engine that turns skills and tools into composable pipelines and safe automations, letting OpenClaw call workflows in one step. 1.1K+ stars.

key_contributions.title: summarize
key_contributions.description: CLI and Chrome Extension that summarizes any URL, YouTube video, podcast, or file. Point at content and get the gist. 5.5K+ GitHub stars, Steinberger's most-starred personal repo.

key_contributions.title: PSPDFKit (Nutrient)
key_contributions.description: PDF rendering SDK co-founded in 2011, fully bootstrapped for 10 years. Powered document workflows at Dropbox, Lufthansa, IBM, and ran on 1B+ Apple devices. Raised EUR 100M+ from Insight Partners in 2021.

key_contributions.title: OpenClaw Ansible
key_contributions.description: Automated, hardened OpenClaw installation with Tailscale VPN, UFW firewall, and Docker isolation for self-hosting. 557 stars.

key_contributions.title: Nix OpenClaw
key_contributions.description: NixOS packages and modules for declarative OpenClaw deployment, reflecting the project's strong Nix community. 644 stars.

quotes.text: My next mission is to build an agent that even my mum can use.
I could totally see how OpenClaw could become a huge company. And no, it's not really exciting for me.
quotes.source: Peter Steinberger, on choosing open source over commercialization

quotes.text: Code is becoming an implementation detail... AI agents don't replace developers. They replace repetitive plumbing.
If you wake up in the morning, and you have nothing to look forward to, you have no real challenge, that gets very boring, very fast.
If you can't understand how to run a command line, this is far too dangerous of a project for you to use safely.
quotes.source: Peter Steinberger, on OpenClaw security responsibilities

key_beliefs: AI replaces repetitive plumbing, not developers
Developers become architects, editors, and prompt engineers
Own your data: run locally, open source, MIT license
technical_philosophy.workflow: Works on Dell 40-inch ultrawide with Ghostty terminal, running 4 simultaneous Claude instances. Orchestrates 5-50 parallel AI agents, merging 600+ commits daily. Dictates prompts by voice.

competitive_landscape.description: OpenClaw occupies a unique position as the most-starred open-source AI personal assistant, differentiated by local-first architecture and messaging platform integration
competitors: Auto-GPT (open-source, but Python-based and less maintained)
moats: 356K+ GitHub stars and massive community momentum
Ecosystem lock-in via ClawhHub skill directory and Lobster workflows
Independent foundation governance ensures long-term community trust
Multi-platform messaging integration (WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, Signal)

sources.title: OpenClaw, OpenAI and the future - Peter Steinberger
Who Is Peter Steinberger? - Spark Agents
OpenClaw creator Peter Steinberger joins OpenAI - TechCrunch
OpenClaw creator joining OpenAI, Altman says - CNBC

questions.question: In 'Finding My Spark Again | Peter Steinberger' you describe rediscovering your motivation after burnout. What specifically about AI-assisted development reignited your drive compared to everything you tried before?
questions.why_this_question: Directly references his blog post about burnout recovery — reveals the emotional arc behind his prolific output
questions.expected_insight: The specific moment or tool that made coding feel new again after years of burnout

questions.question: You wrote 'How We Work at PSPDFKit | Peter Steinberger' about how a visa wait became a career-defining moment. After building PSPDFKit for a decade, what made you walk away — and what brought you back to shipping at this intensity?
questions.why_this_question: References his own blog post about the visa-to-PSPDFKit origin story — connects past and present identity
questions.expected_insight: How his relationship with building software fundamentally changed between PSPDFKit and the AI era

questions.question: In 'Peekaboo MCP – lightning-fast macOS screenshots for AI agents | Peter Steinberger' you detail building MCP infrastructure for AI agents. What's the hardest design tradeoff you've hit — where agent convenience conflicts with system safety?
questions.why_this_question: References his specific MCP work — surfaces real engineering tensions in agent-computer interaction
questions.expected_insight: Concrete examples of where giving agents more capability creates security or reliability risks

questions.question: You ship CLI tools at extraordinary velocity — Poltergeist, Demark, Trimmy, dozens more. When you're building a new tool in a single session, what's your actual decision process for what to keep vs. cut?
questions.why_this_question: Probes the rapid-shipping methodology behind his prolific output
questions.expected_insight: The specific heuristics and shortcuts that enable shipping full tools in hours

questions.question: You draw a sharp line between 'vibe coding' and 'agentic engineering.' Most people conflate them. What's the specific failure mode you've seen when someone treats agent-driven development as just faster autocomplete?
questions.why_this_question: References his core philosophical distinction — this is his signature thesis
questions.expected_insight: A concrete story of agentic engineering done wrong, and what 'right' looks like

questions.question: You describe yourself as 'polyagentmorous' — using multiple AI models simultaneously. Most developers are loyal to one model. What's your actual model-selection process for a given task, and when does multi-model create more noise than signal?
questions.why_this_question: His GitHub profile term 'polyagentmorous' deserves unpacking — it's a real workflow choice
questions.expected_insight: Practical multi-model strategy beyond the meme, including when it fails

questions.question: 'Command your Claude Code Army, Reloaded | Peter Steinberger' describes orchestrating multiple Claude Code instances. You're essentially managing a team of AI agents. How does that compare to managing the human engineering team at PSPDFKit?
questions.why_this_question: Connects his management experience to his agent-orchestration workflow — unique perspective
questions.expected_insight: How managing humans vs. AI agents differs in practice — where each is harder

questions.question: In 'Hiring a distributed team | Peter Steinberger' you wrote about building a distributed team across time zones. Now you're a solo developer with AI agents as your team. What do you miss about human collaborators that no AI can replace?
questions.why_this_question: Probes the human cost of the solo-with-AI model he champions
questions.expected_insight: Honest reflection on what's lost when you replace a team with agents

questions.question: In 'OpenClaw, OpenAI and the future | Peter Steinberger' you explore where AI development is heading. You joined OpenAI to 'build an agent even my mum can use.' What specific interaction pattern are you betting on — and what current approaches are dead ends?
questions.why_this_question: References his stated mission at OpenAI — forces specificity about the product vision
questions.expected_insight: Concrete UX predictions for personal agents, not vague 'AI will change everything'

questions.question: You shipped 684,000 GitHub contributions in one year. At what point does AI-assisted velocity hit diminishing returns — where shipping faster stops being the bottleneck?
questions.why_this_question: His extreme output volume raises questions about whether speed is always the goal
questions.expected_insight: Where the real bottleneck shifts to — design, distribution, user research, or something else

questions.question: In 'Automatic Observation Tracking in UIKit and AppKit: The Feature Apple Forgot to Mention | Peter Steinberger' you document fixing what Apple ships broken. After years of patching UIKit and SwiftUI, what's the most important lesson about working around platform constraints that still applies in the AI era?
questions.why_this_question: His prolific iOS bug-fixing posts reveal a philosophy about shipping despite platform limitations
questions.expected_insight: Whether the 'fix it yourself' iOS mindset transfers to AI-assisted development

questions.question: You wrote 'Automatic Observation Tracking in UIKit and AppKit: The Feature Apple Forgot to Mention | Peter Steinberger' about deep SwiftUI and UIKit internals. Now you build mostly with AI agents writing the code. Has that changed your relationship with framework-level debugging — do you still go as deep?
questions.why_this_question: Explores whether AI-assisted development changes the depth of platform knowledge needed
questions.expected_insight: How the shift from framework expert to AI-assisted builder changes debugging practices

questions.question: You wrote 'InterposeKit — Elegant Swizzling in Swift | Peter Steinberger' and built InterposeKit for elegant method swizzling. That's deep Objective-C runtime work. Does any of that low-level runtime knowledge matter when AI writes most of the code — or is it actually more important?
questions.why_this_question: Tests whether deep platform expertise is becoming more or less valuable with AI
questions.expected_insight: The value of runtime-level understanding when AI handles implementation but not architecture

questions.question: 'Swifty Objective-C | Peter Steinberger' explores bridging Objective-C and Swift idiomatically. You've lived through every Swift migration pain point. What's the AI-era equivalent of the ObjC-to-Swift transition?
questions.why_this_question: Draws a parallel between past language transitions and the current AI-coding shift
questions.expected_insight: Which historical pattern best predicts how AI-assisted development evolves

questions.question: 'How to macOS Core Dump | Peter Steinberger' is a masterclass in low-level debugging. When you're pair-programming with an AI agent now, how do you debug together — does the agent help with the gnarly kernel-level stuff or just get in the way?
questions.why_this_question: His hardcore debugging expertise meets AI-assisted development — a revealing intersection
questions.expected_insight: Where AI helps vs. hinders in deep debugging scenarios

questions.question: In 'Running tests with Clang Address Sanitizer | Peter Steinberger' you tackled test infrastructure at scale. You migrated 700+ tests to Swift Testing. Now you ship projects in hours. What does testing look like when your build-test-ship cycle is measured in minutes?
questions.why_this_question: Explores how rapid AI-assisted development changes testing philosophy
questions.expected_insight: Whether traditional test-driven development survives in the age of AI-speed shipping

questions.question: You wrote 'Using ccache for Fun and Profit | Peter Steinberger' about optimizing build infrastructure. You've spent years fighting Xcode build times with ccache and zld. Now AI generates code faster than compilers can build it. What's the new bottleneck?
questions.why_this_question: His deep build-system expertise creates a unique lens on AI-speed development
questions.expected_insight: Whether build infrastructure becomes more or less important when AI writes the code

questions.question: In 'Code Signing and Notarization: Sparkle and Tears | Peter Steinberger' you describe the pain of code signing and notarization. You fought Apple's distribution requirements for years. Now you ship open-source tools globally. How has your relationship with Apple's walled garden changed?
questions.why_this_question: His code signing struggles are legendary in the iOS community — connects to his current open-source focus
questions.expected_insight: Whether the move from commercial SDK to open-source AI tools is partly a reaction to platform constraints

questions.question: In 'Updating macOS on a Hackintosh | Peter Steinberger' you explored pushing Apple hardware and software to its limits. You've jailbroken iPhones, built Hackintoshes, and fought kernel_task. That hacker mentality — does it show up in how you approach AI agents?
questions.why_this_question: Connects his hardware-hacking roots to his current agent-orchestration work
questions.expected_insight: Whether the 'break it to understand it' mindset applies to AI development

questions.question: You wrote 'Showing Settings from macOS Menu Bar Items: A 5-Hour Journey | Peter Steinberger' about bringing iOS apps to the Mac. You lived through Catalyst, Marzipan, and SwiftUI's cross-platform promise. Now you build cross-platform with TypeScript and AI. What did Apple get wrong about cross-platform?
questions.why_this_question: His unique perspective spanning Apple's cross-platform attempts and modern AI-native development
questions.expected_insight: What Apple's cross-platform failures teach about the AI-native approach to multi-platform

questions.question: In 'llm.codes: Make Apple Docs AI-Readable | Peter Steinberger' you describe your daily AI development setup. You've built Vibe Meter, VibeTunnel, llm.codes, stats.store — an entire ecosystem around AI-assisted coding. When do your own tools surprise you vs. when do they fall short?
questions.why_this_question: He's uniquely positioned as both a power user and tool builder in the AI dev space
questions.expected_insight: Where the gap is between the tools that exist and the tools AI-native developers actually need

questions.question: 'Live Coding Session: Building Arena | Peter Steinberger' shows you building and shipping live with AI. You've done multiple live coding sessions where you build entire apps in one sitting. What's the biggest difference between shipping solo on stream vs. shipping solo in private?
questions.why_this_question: His live coding sessions reveal the raw, unfiltered process — including failures
questions.expected_insight: How public accountability and audience feedback change the AI-assisted development process

questions.question: You wrote 'Gardening Your Twitter: Growing Your Followers | Peter Steinberger' about strategically building your Twitter audience. You now have 385K followers. How much of your current influence comes from that deliberate social media strategy vs. just shipping impressive things?
questions.why_this_question: His Twitter gardening posts predate his AI fame — reveals how deliberate his brand-building has been
questions.expected_insight: The relationship between strategic audience-building and organic virality from shipping

questions.question: In 'Don't Call willChangeValueForKey Unless It's Really Needed | Peter Steinberger' you captured the details that most developers skip — the deep iOS internals, the edge cases, the undocumented behaviors. Now you blog about AI. Has your writing process changed, or is it the same obsessive detail applied to a new domain?
questions.why_this_question: Connects his meticulous iOS blogging to his current AI-focused writing
questions.expected_insight: Whether the blogging craft transfers across domains or requires reinvention
